misconceptions.misconception: Recursion needs a loop inside it to make things repeat.
misconceptions.correction: Recursion repeats by having the function call ITSELF on a smaller version of the problem. The recursive call is what creates the repetition - no loop is needed inside the function body.

misconceptions.misconception: The base case is the first line of a recursive function and always runs before anything else.
misconceptions.correction: The base case is the TERMINATING condition that stops recursion and returns directly. It runs only when the input is small enough to answer without recursing further; on larger inputs the recursive case runs first.

misconceptions.misconception: When you reassign a variable, Python keeps the old value accessible as an attribute.
misconceptions.correction: When you reassign a variable in Python, the name is simply rebound to the new value. The old value is no longer accessible through that name and is garbage-collected if no other reference exists.

misconceptions.misconception: A Python variable is a box that holds a value.
misconceptions.correction: A Python variable is a NAME that REFERS to an object in memory, not a container that holds it. Multiple variables can refer to the same object (a = [1]; b = a).

misconceptions.misconception: for loops can only iterate over lists.
misconceptions.correction: for loops iterate over ANY iterable: lists, tuples, strings, dicts, sets, ranges, generators, file objects, and any object that implements __iter__.

misconceptions.misconception: A while loop always runs at least once.
misconceptions.correction: A while loop checks its condition BEFORE the first iteration. If the condition is False initially, the loop body never runs. You may be thinking of do-while loops in other languages; Python has no do-while.

misconceptions.misconception: Python lists have a fixed size like arrays in C or Java.
misconceptions.correction: Python lists are dynamic. You can append, insert, or remove elements at any time. There is no need to declare a size upfront.

misconceptions.misconception: Negative indices throw an IndexError in Python.
misconceptions.correction: Negative indices count from the END of the sequence: list[-1] is the last element, list[-2] the second to last, and so on. They only raise IndexError if their absolute value exceeds the length.

misconceptions.misconception: Dictionary keys must be strings.
misconceptions.correction: Dictionary keys can be any HASHABLE object: strings, numbers, tuples, frozensets, None, booleans, and more. They cannot be mutable types like lists, dicts, or sets.

misconceptions.misconception: Tuples are just lists you can not change - there is no other meaningful difference.
misconceptions.correction: Beyond immutability, tuples are hashable (can be dict keys / set members), use less memory, are slightly faster, and signal intent: a tuple typically means 'a fixed-shape grouping' (like coordinates) while a list means 'a collection of similar items'.

misconceptions.misconception: You can change a character in a string by assigning to an index: s[0] = 'A'.
misconceptions.correction: Strings in Python are IMMUTABLE. To change a character you must build a new string, e.g. s = 'A' + s[1:].

misconceptions.misconception: Functions must return a value, otherwise they crash.
misconceptions.correction: Functions without a return statement (or with a bare 'return') implicitly return None. This is perfectly valid; many functions exist for side effects (print, list.append) and intentionally return None.

misconceptions.misconception: Code after a return statement still runs.
misconceptions.correction: A return statement immediately exits the function. Any code after it inside that branch is unreachable. (try/finally is the only exception: finally blocks run even after a return.)

misconceptions.misconception: Variables declared inside a function are accessible anywhere in the program.
misconceptions.correction: Variables defined inside a function are LOCAL to that function. Outside code cannot see them. To share data, pass arguments in or return values out.

misconceptions.misconception: A lambda can have multiple statements separated by semicolons.
misconceptions.correction: A lambda must contain a SINGLE expression. For multiple statements or any statement (if/for/print/etc), use a normal def function.

misconceptions.misconception: Linear search is the fastest way to find an element in a sequence.
misconceptions.correction: Linear search is O(n) and only optimal for unsorted data or very small inputs. If the data is sorted, binary search at O(log n) is dramatically faster on large inputs.

misconceptions.misconception: Binary search works on any list, regardless of order.
misconceptions.correction: Binary search requires the input to be SORTED. On unsorted input it may miss the element entirely. If you need fast lookup on unsorted data, build a set or dict first.

misconceptions.misconception: An O(n) algorithm is always faster than an O(n^2) algorithm.
misconceptions.correction: Big O ignores constants. For small inputs an O(n^2) algorithm with low constants can be faster than an O(n) one with high constants. Big O describes growth as n becomes large, not speed at any specific n.

misconceptions.misconception: Adding more lines of code makes a function slower in Big O terms.
misconceptions.correction: Big O measures how many operations grow WITH THE INPUT SIZE, not the line count. A 100-line function that does one pass over the data is O(n); a 5-line function with a nested loop is O(n^2).

misconceptions.misconception: try/except is used to catch syntax errors in your code.
misconceptions.correction: Syntax errors are detected BEFORE the program runs and cannot be caught with try/except. try/except handles RUNTIME exceptions: ValueError, TypeError, KeyError, ZeroDivisionError, IOError, and so on.
